common_name: Truckers Favorite White Corn (Market Garden White Dent Corn)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 60°F; Truckers Favorite White is one of the most historically significant Southern market garden corns — grown for over 150 years by market farmers (the 'truckers' who hauled produce to market) for its excellent fresh-eating quality at the roasting ear stage, large white kernels, tall productive plants, and reliable performance in the hot, humid summer conditions of the South.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in blocks of at least 4 rows for good pollination; thin to 8–12 inches; Truckers Favorite is tall and produces 1–2 ears per stalk; harvest at the milk stage (when kernels exude a milky liquid when punctured) for best fresh-eating quality; allow to dry on the stalk for grinding into grits or cornmeal. Truckers Favorite White Corn is a classic Southern open-pollinated white dent corn that has been grown in the American South since before the Civil War and takes its name from the small-scale produce farmers ('truckers') who grew and hauled vegetables to regional markets throughout the 19th and early 20th centuries — the same context that gave rise to terms like 'truck farming' and 'market gardening.' The variety was selected over generations specifically for the combination of early-maturing roasting ear quality (harvested at the milk stage for fresh eating) and excellent flour/grits quality when allowed to dry fully. The white kernels are large and doughy at the roasting ear stage, sweet enough for fresh eating but not as sugary as modern sweet corn varieties — the characteristic semi-sweet, starchy quality that old-fashioned cornbread and grits recipes were designed around. In the Gulf South, Truckers Favorite White is particularly valued as a heritage Southern cornbread corn — ground into coarse grits or fine meal, it produces the authentic white stone-ground cornmeal preferred in traditional Southern cooking. Plants are tall (7–9 feet), productive, and heat tolerant, maturing in the relatively modest time of 85–95 days that suits the Gulf Coast growing season.

common_name: Cherokee White Eagle Corn (Native American Flint-Dent White Corn)

planting.note: Direct sow in blocks of at least 4 rows after last frost when soil reaches 60°F; Cherokee White Eagle is a Cherokee Nation heirloom corn maintained by members of the Cherokee people across the original homelands of the southern Appalachians and Ozarks — it is a white flour/dent-type corn notable for occasional dark kernels in the cob that create an eagle-wing pattern, a characteristic considered sacred in Cherokee tradition.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in blocks for pollination; allow ears to fully dry on the stalk before harvest for the best grain quality; Cherokee White Eagle makes excellent hominy (nixtamalized corn), grits, and cornbread; select seed from ears showing the traditional dark-kernel eagle pattern to maintain the variety's distinctive characteristic. Cherokee White Eagle is a corn variety maintained and revered by the Cherokee people, one of the Five Civilized Tribes of the American Southeast (originally from the mountains of North Carolina, Tennessee, and Georgia), who grew multiple varieties of corn as a primary food staple for centuries before European contact. The corn takes its name from the visual pattern that occasionally appears on a cob: most kernels are white, but scattered dark blue-black or red-black kernels create a pattern that, when viewed on the ear, suggests the spread wings of an eagle — a symbol of great importance in Cherokee culture. The proportion and distribution of dark kernels varies among individual ears, and Cherokee tradition holds that cobs with the most pronounced eagle pattern should be selected for seed saving to perpetuate both the variety and its spiritual significance. As a grain, Cherokee White Eagle is a flour/dent-type corn producing large, soft, white kernels that grind into fine, flavorful cornmeal and are excellent for making hominy (the traditional Indigenous preparation where corn is nixtamalized with alkali — wood ash or lime — to improve nutritional availability). The variety adapts well to the heat and humidity of the southeastern United States where it was developed.

common_name: Floriani Red Flint Corn (Italian Polenta Corn, Mais Rosso della Val di Fiemme)

planting.note: Direct sow in blocks of at least 4 rows after last frost when soil has warmed; Floriani Red Flint is a long-season Italian heirloom (100–120 days) requiring a full summer to mature — in short-season climates (zones 3–5) start a week or two early under plastic mulch; this is the finest polenta corn available, producing deeply colored red-orange kernels that grind into a rich, nutty, complex polenta unequaled by commercial varieties.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in blocks; allow ears to dry fully on the stalks until the husks are completely papery before harvest; dry further at low heat (under 100°F) to bring moisture content below 12% before milling; stone-grind for the finest polenta texture; the distinctive deep red-orange color of the meal is unique among American corns. Floriani Red Flint is widely considered one of the finest polenta corns in the world — an Italian heirloom from the Fiemme Valley (Val di Fiemme) of Trentino, in the northeastern Italian Alps, where this particular strain of red flint corn has been grown by mountain farmers for centuries and is deeply embedded in the local food culture. The name 'Floriani' commemorates the Floriani family who maintained and preserved this specific strain. The corn produces large, deeply pigmented red-orange to brick-red flint kernels with a very hard, glassy endosperm characteristic of flint corn — the same endosperm structure that gives flint corns their superior milling quality and longer shelf life compared to dent corns. When stone-ground, the flour from Floriani Red Flint is a rich deep gold-orange color with a complex, nutty, slightly sweet fragrance quite unlike the pale, bland commercial cornmeal most American cooks know. Cooked polenta from this corn has a dense, complex flavor with depth that fully justifies the artisan price of stone-ground Italian polenta. Chefs at farm-to-table restaurants have sought out this variety specifically for its polenta quality. American growers in zones with a full summer can produce the authentic Italian-quality polenta grain that was formerly available only through import.

common_name: Otto File Corn (Tuscan Eight-Row Corn, Otto Fila Giallo)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in blocks of at least 4 rows; Otto File is a Tuscan yellow flint corn named for its defining characteristic — exactly eight rows of kernels on each ear (otto = eight, file = rows in Italian) — and the traditional grain for authentic Tuscan polenta, where its deep yellow color and nutty flavor produce a superior porridge distinct from the pale commercial product.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in well-prepared, fertile soil; Otto File is an eight-row flint corn that produces compact ears with exactly 8 rows of golden-yellow kernels; allow ears to dry fully on the stalk; stone-grind for the authentic coarse-textured, deeply flavored polenta that is standard in Tuscany and distinguishes Italian-style polenta from American cornmeal mush. Otto File (Italian: otto fila = eight rows) is the traditional polenta corn of Tuscany, the Italian region where polenta — while less dominant than in the Venice region of northeastern Italy — has been made from this specific eight-row flint corn for generations. The variety name precisely describes the defining characteristic of the ear: each cob bears exactly eight rows of kernels, a consistent genetic trait that is visible at harvest and that Italian farmers used for centuries as a way to identify and maintain the variety. The yellow flint kernels are deeply golden to amber-yellow at maturity, and when stone-ground they produce a meal with a rich, nutty, complex flavor and a pleasantly coarse, somewhat granular texture that gives polenta its characteristic hearty body. Otto File is shorter-season than the Floriani Red Flint (90–110 days versus 100–120) and adapts well to a wide range of climates including the humid Southeast and Gulf South. The compact plant height (5–7 feet) makes it somewhat easier to manage in wind-prone garden situations than taller heritage corns. Otto File is prized by Italian-American home cooks and professional chefs who seek the authentic grain for traditional polenta preparations.

common_name: Blue Jade Sweet Corn (Dwarf Blue Sweet Corn, Container Corn)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 60°F; Blue Jade is unique among sweet corns for its genuinely dwarf plant size (3–4 feet tall) that makes it the most practical sweet corn for container growing, small raised beds, and gardens where standard 6–8 foot corn stalks are not practical; the vivid blue to near-black kernels turn steel-blue when cooked and have a sweet, tender flavor despite their striking color.

directions: Direct sow in blocks of at least 4 rows for pollination — even dwarf corn requires block planting; thin to 6–9 inches; Blue Jade can be grown in large containers (15+ gallons) for patio gardens; harvest when kernels exude milky liquid when punctured; Blue Jade silk is blue and the husk has blue streaks; eat immediately for best sweetness as the sugars convert rapidly. Blue Jade is the most practical sweet corn for small gardens and containers — a genuinely dwarf sweet corn variety (plants only 3–4 feet tall) that was selected specifically for its compact habit, making it the rare sweet corn that can be grown in large containers, small raised beds, or urban gardens where standard corn's height and space requirements are prohibitive. The kernels are a vivid blue-purple when raw, turning a distinctive slate-blue when cooked — a color unlike any other common sweet corn, derived from the same anthocyanin pigments found in blue tortilla corn, blueberries, and purple cabbage. Despite the striking color, the flavor at the perfect milky stage is sweet and tender, comparable to standard sweet corn, though with a slightly more complex, starchy background flavor reflecting the corn's partial flint heritage. Blue Jade is classified as a sugary-enhanced (se) type sweet corn — not as sugary as supersweet (sh2) types but sweeter than old su types, with a longer window between milk and dough stages than original open-pollinated sweet corns. The entire plant is attractive: blue silk, blue-streaked husks, and blue-purple stalk coloring make Blue Jade an ornamental as well as productive corn. Excellent for children's gardens for its manageable size and dramatic appearance.
